drift_findings.description: Two distinct checklists share the root phrase 'three-question check'. Topic 2.6 labels its AI-suitability decision tool 'the three-question check' (privacy / precision / legal accountability). Topic 2.8 labels its output-testing tool 'the three-question verification check'. Topic 2.9 refers back to 2.8 as 'the three-question check from topic 2.8', reusing the shorter form that 2.6 already owns. A beginner two topics past 2.6 reading 2.9 will encounter the same phrase pointing at a different tool. Not resolved since cycle 2.
drift_findings.recommendation: Rename the 2.6 tool throughout its corpus to 'AI suitability check (privacy, precision, accountability)' or '3-gate AI suitability check'. Reserve 'three-question verification check' exclusively for 2.8 and 2.9.

drift_findings.description: Topic 2.4 corpus (section 2, Prerequisites) lists '1.4 — Pattern recognition (surface introduction): the idea that humans and machines look for regularities.' Topic 1.4's actual title is 'Why AI gives different answers to the same question' and its concepts_introduced are: LLM (Large Language Model), token, token sampling, probability distribution. Topic 1.4 does not introduce pattern recognition at any level. The description in 2.4's corpus is factually wrong and will confuse a learner who cross-references back to 1.4 seeking the pattern-recognition foundation that 2.4 claims was taught there.
drift_findings.recommendation: Correct 2.4's prerequisites section. If pattern recognition does appear in 1.4's reading artifact implicitly, that is a scope-creep issue to resolve in 1.4. If not, remove the pattern-recognition claim from 2.4's prereq description and note instead: '1.3 — Probabilistic systems' or '1.4 — Why AI gives different answers to the same question (probabilistic output, token sampling)' without the erroneous subject-matter label.

drift_findings.description: 'Iterative refinement' introduced in 1.5 (Decomposition, task-tree refinement context). 'Iteration cycle' introduced in 2.9 (spec-testing loop context). Both use root 'iterate/iterative' in non-contradictory ways. No redefinition occurs. A one-sentence bridge in 2.9 connecting to the 1.5 usage would strengthen continuity. No action required.
drift_findings.recommendation: Optional: add one sentence in 2.9 Introduction noting the iterative-improvement habit first seen in 1.5 decomposition applies here.

notes.description: Topics 2.1, 2.2, 2.3, and 2.4 retain empty prerequisites arrays in sequence.index.json. Their corpora declare valid week-1 and intra-week-2 dependencies. This is underdeclaration only — no forward reference exists. Automated downstream tooling that relies solely on sequence.index.json prerequisites will miss these dependencies.
notes.recommendation: Populate prerequisites in sequence.index.json to match corpus prereq sections. For 2.1: ["1.1", "1.9", "1.7"]. For 2.2: ["2.1"]. For 2.3: ["2.1", "2.2"]. For 2.4: ["1.3", "1.4", "2.1", "2.3"].

per_week.notes: All 12 topics have delivery minutes. 84 min is -6.7% from 90-min target, within ±15% soft-warn band.
All 9 topics have delivery minutes. 78 min is -13.3% from 90-min target, within ±15% soft-warn band. Six deep topics (2.4–2.9) at 10 min each; three standard topics (2.1–2.3) at 6 min each.

weekly_time_budget.overall_budget_verdict: PASS — Week 1: 84 min (ok, -6.7%). Week 2: 78 min (ok, -13.3%). All 21 topics have delivery minutes. No week falls outside the ±15% soft-warn band.

notable_near_pairs.description: Both topics discuss probabilistic outputs, but 1.3 covers the definitional model (same input → range of outputs, sampling, temperature) while 2.4 advances to the machine-learning mechanism (training on labeled data, features, classification, confidence scores). Vocabulary overlap ('probabilistic', 'output variation') is intentional bridging. Well below 0.55 threshold.

Both topics emphasise unambiguous, precise instructions. 1.9 covers algorithm properties; 2.1 covers specification properties (testable, bounded, observable, actionable). Shared vocabulary around 'precision' and 'unambiguous' is appropriate cross-reference. Below threshold.

coverage_map.evidence: Topic 1.5 LOs: define decomposition, identify parts of a problem, produce a task tree, apply decomposition to a chosen problem.
Topic 1.6 LOs: define abstraction, explain hiding complexity, identify right level of detail, distinguish good vs leaky abstraction.
Topic 1.7 LOs: explain pseudocode, write basic pseudocode, identify sequence/decision/repetition building blocks.

2.1 introduces four-property checklist (testable/bounded/observable/actionable); 2.2 practises diagnosing and rewriting bad specs; 2.3 teaches input/output/failure-condition identification; 2.7 applies spec writing across five real domains; 2.8 teaches testing a spec against AI output; 2.9 teaches iterating a spec based on output gaps.

verdict: PASS — all 5 checks pass. Two warn findings in terminology consistency (three-question naming collision unresolved since cycle 2; 2.4 corpus mislabeled prerequisite description for topic 1.4) and one info finding. Neither is a hard fail. Weekly time-budget: week 1 total 84 min (ok, -6.7%), week 2 total 78 min (ok, -13.3%). No null delivery minutes. No cross-week Jaccard pairs above 0.55. All four course scope facets covered.

open_warn_items.summary: Three-question naming collision: 'three-question check' (2.6 suitability) vs 'three-question verification check' (2.8 output testing). Recommend renaming 2.6 tool.

Corpus section 2 describes prereq 1.4 as 'Pattern recognition (surface introduction)' — factually wrong. Topic 1.4 covers LLMs/token sampling/probability distribution, not pattern recognition. Correct 2.4's prereq description.
